texts.label: text
texts.orig: CKD is the presence of one of the following for>3 months.
texts.text: CKD is the presence of one of the following for>3 months.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Offer Screening for CKD using eGFR, serum creatinine and Urine Albumin: Creatinine
texts.text: Offer Screening for CKD using eGFR, serum creatinine and Urine Albumin: Creatinine

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ratio (UACR) to people with any of the following risk factors:
texts.text: Ratio (UACR) to people with any of the following risk factors:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: All people living with diabetes at least annually.
texts.text: All people living with diabetes at least annually.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For those with an eGFR<60ml/min/1.73m2 a UACR should be requested
texts.text: For those with an eGFR<60ml/min/1.73m2 a UACR should be requested

texts.label: text

texts.text: Hypertension-annually as part of hypertensions reviews

texts.text: arterial disease or cerebral vascular disease) annually as part of routine reviews

texts.label: text

texts.text: History of acute kidney injury (monitor yearly for 3 years even if function back to

texts.label: text

texts.text: Structural renal tract disease, recurrent renal calculi or prostatic hypertrophy

texts.label: text

texts.text: Multi-system disease e.g., Systemic lupus erythematosus, vasculitis, myeloma

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Family history of end-stage kidney disease (GFR category G5) or hereditary kidney
texts.text: Family history of end-stage kidney disease (GFR category G5) or hereditary kidney

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Treated with nephro-toxic agents (NSAIDs, Lithium, Calcineurin inhibitors,
texts.text: Treated with nephro-toxic agents (NSAIDs, Lithium, Calcineurin inhibitors,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Albuminuria (UACR ≥3 mg/mmol) confirmed on an early
texts.text: Albuminuria (UACR ≥3 mg/mmol) confirmed on an early

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Urine sediment abnormalities e.g., presence of red (could
texts.text: Urine sediment abnormalities e.g., presence of red (could

texts.label: text
texts.orig: indicate glomerular disease) or white blood cells (could
texts.text: indicate glomerular disease) or white blood cells (could

texts.label: text
texts.orig: indicate interstitial nephritis or infection e.g. pyelonephritis) ,
texts.text: indicate interstitial nephritis or infection e.g. pyelonephritis) ,

texts.text: tubular epithelial cells (could indicate parenchymal disease)

texts.text: Electrolyte and other abnormalities due to tubular disorders

texts.label: text
texts.orig: eGFR of <60 ml/min/1.73 m2 (eGFR categories G3a-G5)
texts.text: eGFR of <60 ml/min/1.73 m2 (eGFR categories G3a-G5)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Every patient at the time of a clinician diagnosing CKD should have a urine dipstick because haematuria raises possibility of systemic renal disease or structural renal abnormalities which needs further assessment.
texts.text: Every patient at the time of a clinician diagnosing CKD should have a urine dipstick because haematuria raises possibility of systemic renal disease or structural renal abnormalities which needs further assessment.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Use dipstick reagent strips rather than urine microscopy.
texts.text: Use dipstick reagent strips rather than urine microscopy.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Evaluate further if a result of 1+ or more (initially repeat dipstick in 2 weeks)
texts.text: Evaluate further if a result of 1+ or more (initially repeat dipstick in 2 weeks)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Result is not useful if the person is menstruating if someone has a catheter or has a known infection.
texts.text: Result is not useful if the person is menstruating if someone has a catheter or has a known infection.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: How do we categorise CKD, how often should we test and when should we refer/seek advice?
texts.text: How do we categorise CKD, how often should we test and when should we refer/seek advice?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When reviewing results, place the test results in clinical context including consideration of why the blood tests were taken.  If history of acute illness, then assess and manage accordingly. Consider acute kidney injury (AKI) and the possibility of obstruction if rapidly declining eGFR. Think Kidneys https://www.thinkkidneys.nhs.uk/aki/resources/primary-care/, https://www.thinkkidneys.nhs.uk/campaign/
texts.text: When reviewing results, place the test results in clinical context including consideration of why the blood tests were taken.  If history of acute illness, then assess and manage accordingly. Consider acute kidney injury (AKI) and the possibility of obstruction if rapidly declining eGFR. Think Kidneys https://www.thinkkidneys.nhs.uk/aki/resources/primary-care/, https://www.thinkkidneys.nhs.uk/campaign/

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Where referral required, this should be to renal services if the patient does not have diabetes, or to combined diabetes/renal clinic for patient with diabetes (unless suspected or known non-diabetic kidney disease or eGFR <20ml/min1.73 m2 in which case referral should be to renal service)
texts.text: Where referral required, this should be to renal services if the patient does not have diabetes, or to combined diabetes/renal clinic for patient with diabetes (unless suspected or known non-diabetic kidney disease or eGFR <20ml/min1.73 m2 in which case referral should be to renal service)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Refer adults with CKD for specialist assessment (considering their wishes and comorbidities) if they have any of the following:
texts.text: Refer adults with CKD for specialist assessment (considering their wishes and comorbidities) if they have any of the following:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · 5-year risk of needing renal replacement therapy of greater than 5% (measured using the 4-variable Kidney Failure Risk Equation )
texts.text: 5-year risk of needing renal replacement therapy of greater than 5% (measured using the 4-variable Kidney Failure Risk Equation )

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · ACR of 70 mg/mmol or more, unless known to be caused by diabetes and already appropriately treated
texts.text: ACR of 70 mg/mmol or more, unless known to be caused by diabetes and already appropriately treated

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: • ACR of more than 30 mg/mmol (ACR category A3), together with haematuria
texts.text: ACR of more than 30 mg/mmol (ACR category A3), together with haematuria

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: • a sustained decrease in eGFR of 25% or more and a change in eGFR category within 12 months
texts.text: a sustained decrease in eGFR of 25% or more and a change in eGFR category within 12 months

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · a sustained decrease in eGFR of 15 ml/min/1.73 m2 or more per year
texts.text: a sustained decrease in eGFR of 15 ml/min/1.73 m2 or more per year

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · hypertension that remains poorly controlled (above the person's individual target) despite the use of at least 4 antihypertensive medicines at therapeutic doses
texts.text: hypertension that remains poorly controlled (above the person's individual target) despite the use of at least 4 antihypertensive medicines at therapeutic doses

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: • known or suspected rare or genetic causes of CKD
texts.text: known or suspected rare or genetic causes of CKD

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·Patients with eGFR <30 ml/min/1.73 m2 will usually require referral; but with eGFR ≥30 ml/min/1.73 m2 referral will depend on other factors as above.
texts.text: ·Patients with eGFR <30 ml/min/1.73 m2 will usually require referral; but with eGFR ≥30 ml/min/1.73 m2 referral will depend on other factors as above.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A&G = Advice and Guidance or refer NB: G1A1 and G2A1 only classed as CKD if also have additional Markers of Kidney Disease e.g. renal stone disease.
texts.text: A&G = Advice and Guidance or refer NB: G1A1 and G2A1 only classed as CKD if also have additional Markers of Kidney Disease e.g. renal stone disease.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3 Step Solutions for the Management of Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)
texts.text: 3 Step Solutions for the Management of Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (ideally do in every patient with eGFR<60 or UACR ≥ 3 mg/mmol)
texts.text: (ideally do in every patient with eGFR<60 or UACR ≥ 3 mg/mmol)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: *RAAS inhibitors-Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors, **ACE inhibitors-Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors ***ARB - Angiotensin Receptor Blocker. RAAS inhibitors include ACEI (e.g. ramipril) and ARB (e.g. losartan).
texts.text: *RAAS inhibitors-Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system inhibitors, **ACE inhibitors-Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors ***ARB - Angiotensin Receptor Blocker. RAAS inhibitors include ACEI (e.g. ramipril) and ARB (e.g. losartan).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Diagnosing and coding CKD early enables people to access interventions such as Lifestyle advice and pharmacotherapy to reduce the risk of CKD progressing and of significant cardiovascular complications.
texts.text: Diagnosing and coding CKD early enables people to access interventions such as Lifestyle advice and pharmacotherapy to reduce the risk of CKD progressing and of significant cardiovascular complications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lifestyle advice - diet, exercise, weight management, smoking cessation, Vaccination-Flu, Pneumococcal
texts.text: Lifestyle advice - diet, exercise, weight management, smoking cessation, Vaccination-Flu, Pneumococcal

texts.label: page_footer
texts.orig: Guidelines for management of CKD in adults
texts.text: Guidelines for management of CKD in adults

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Assess if patient suitable for rapid RAAS titration (unsuitable if low baseline blood pressure, people with significant co- morbidities, potassium level already near ULN)
texts.text: Assess if patient suitable for rapid RAAS titration (unsuitable if low baseline blood pressure, people with significant co- morbidities, potassium level already near ULN)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If not consider reducing any other BP medications and where appropriate setting frailty targets for blood pressure
texts.text: If not consider reducing any other BP medications and where appropriate setting frailty targets for blood pressure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider a slower titration of RAAS blockade and consider a reduced dose at initiation
texts.text: Consider a slower titration of RAAS blockade and consider a reduced dose at initiation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Start ACE inhibitor (or ARB if ACE I not tolerated) at half maximum dose e.g., Ramipril 5mg once daily
texts.text: Start ACE inhibitor (or ARB if ACE I not tolerated) at half maximum dose e.g., Ramipril 5mg once daily

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: - Advise on good fluid intake 1-2L/day depending on size.
texts.text: Advise on good fluid intake 1-2L/day depending on size.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Check creatinine and potassium 1-2 weeks after initiation and recheck blood pressure.
texts.text: Check creatinine and potassium 1-2 weeks after initiation and recheck blood pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Increase ACE inhibitor (or ARB if ACEi not tolerated) to maximum tolerated dose e.g., Ramipril 10mg od.
texts.text: Increase ACE inhibitor (or ARB if ACEi not tolerated) to maximum tolerated dose e.g., Ramipril 10mg od.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Check creatinine and potassium 1-2 weeks after each dose titration and recheck blood pressure.
texts.text: Check creatinine and potassium 1-2 weeks after each dose titration and recheck blood pressure.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Rapid Titration Protocol for RAAS Blockade
texts.text: Rapid Titration Protocol for RAAS Blockade

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At month 4 onwards consider Finerenone for people with Type 2 Diabetes and who also has:
texts.text: At month 4 onwards consider Finerenone for people with Type 2 Diabetes and who also has:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · stage 3 or 4 CKD (eGFR ≥25 -<60 ml/min with albuminuria (UACR ≥3mg/mmol) and
texts.text: stage 3 or 4 CKD (eGFR ≥25 -<60 ml/min with albuminuria (UACR ≥3mg/mmol) and

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · been optimised on standard care (RAAS blockade and SGLT2 inhibitors) unless unsuitable.
texts.text: been optimised on standard care (RAAS blockade and SGLT2 inhibitors) unless unsuitable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The starting dose is 10mg once daily. The recommended target dose is 20mg once daily.
texts.text: The starting dose is 10mg once daily. The recommended target dose is 20mg once daily.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Refer to APC Finerenone guidelines for further information on treatment initiation, continuation, dose adjustment and monitoring.
texts.text: Refer to APC Finerenone guidelines for further information on treatment initiation, continuation, dose adjustment and monitoring.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Accept a serum creatinine rise < 30% or eGFR fall of < 25% from baseline: after ACEi/ARB initiation or dose increase. Avoid initiating ACEI/ARB and SGLT2 inhibitors together as it can have a cumulative effect of <30%. If renal function deterioration greater than stated above seek nephrologist advice (to exclude possible reno-vascular disease)
texts.text: Accept a serum creatinine rise < 30% or eGFR fall of < 25% from baseline: after ACEi/ARB initiation or dose increase. Avoid initiating ACEI/ARB and SGLT2 inhibitors together as it can have a cumulative effect of <30%. If renal function deterioration greater than stated above seek nephrologist advice (to exclude possible reno-vascular disease)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: STOP ACEi/ARB if changes in creatinine/ eGFR exceed the above and no other causes of deteriorating renal function (e.g., dehydration, use of NSAIDs) is found.
texts.text: STOP ACEi/ARB if changes in creatinine/ eGFR exceed the above and no other causes of deteriorating renal function (e.g., dehydration, use of NSAIDs) is found.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If K + >6.0 mmol/L -would need urgent repeat U&E (please follow local guidance and ideally this would be a same day repeat) and if 6.5 mmol/L or greater or if there are symptoms consistent with hyperkalaemia, you would usually send to A&E for repeat potassium and ECG. If K + >6.0 mmol/L stop ACEi/ARB and start low potassium diet, a recommended patient information can be found: https://www.kidney.org.uk/potassium.
texts.text: If K + >6.0 mmol/L -would need urgent repeat U&E (please follow local guidance and ideally this would be a same day repeat) and if 6.5 mmol/L or greater or if there are symptoms consistent with hyperkalaemia, you would usually send to A&E for repeat potassium and ECG. If K + >6.0 mmol/L stop ACEi/ARB and start low potassium diet, a recommended patient information can be found: https://www.kidney.org.uk/potassium.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If K+ remains persistently ≥6.0mmol/L and because of this hyperkalaemia people are unable to take an optimised dose of RAAS inhibitor. consider referral for sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (for CKD stage 3b-5, not on dialysis only)
texts.text: If K+ remains persistently ≥6.0mmol/L and because of this hyperkalaemia people are unable to take an optimised dose of RAAS inhibitor. consider referral for sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (for CKD stage 3b-5, not on dialysis only)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Aim to restart medications once K + ≤ 5.5 mmol/L (note lower starting doses with Finerenone below)
texts.text: Aim to restart medications once K + ≤ 5.5 mmol/L (note lower starting doses with Finerenone below)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the patient has proteinuria or heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and would benefit from an ACEi/ARB seek nephrologist advice as introduction of furosemide, potassium binders or bicarbonate to facilitate reintroduction of these agents.
texts.text: If the patient has proteinuria or heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and would benefit from an ACEi/ARB seek nephrologist advice as introduction of furosemide, potassium binders or bicarbonate to facilitate reintroduction of these agents.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Concomitant use of ACEi/ARB with spironolactone and other potassium sparing diuretics requires close monitoring of potassium.  The Think Kidneys campaign has a useful guidance which can be found 2020-statement-on-Changes-inKidney-Function-FINAL.pdf (thinkkidneys.nhs.uk)
texts.text: Concomitant use of ACEi/ARB with spironolactone and other potassium sparing diuretics requires close monitoring of potassium.  The Think Kidneys campaign has a useful guidance which can be found 2020-statement-on-Changes-inKidney-Function-FINAL.pdf (thinkkidneys.nhs.uk)

texts.text: ·TIMELY REFERRAL to secondary care of those at risk of progression to end stage renal disease

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Kidneydamagedefinedby: Markersofdamage Pathologicalabnormalities Bloodtests creatinineratio>3mg/mmol) Albuminuria(urinealbuminto Haemoproteinuriainabsenceof UTI Abnormal Imagingstudies
texts.text: Kidneydamagedefinedby: Markersofdamage Pathologicalabnormalities Bloodtests creatinineratio>3mg/mmol) Albuminuria(urinealbuminto Haemoproteinuriainabsenceof UTI Abnormal Imagingstudies

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •Statin: Atorvastatin 20mg OD  for primary and secondary prevention of CVD
texts.text: •Statin: Atorvastatin 20mg OD  for primary and secondary prevention of CVD

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·T2DM only: Dapagliflozin, Canagliflozin, Empagliflozin, (Ertugliflozin Offer if eGFR ≥ 60)
texts.text: ·T2DM only: Dapagliflozin, Canagliflozin, Empagliflozin, (Ertugliflozin Offer if eGFR ≥ 60)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •T2DM and eGFR 20-90: Dapagliflozin  OR Empagliflozin , Canagliflozin if uACR >30 mg/mmol and consider if uACR 3-30 mg/mmol
texts.text: •T2DM and eGFR 20-90: Dapagliflozin  OR Empagliflozin , Canagliflozin if uACR >30 mg/mmol and consider if uACR 3-30 mg/mmol

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·CKD if eGFR ≥ 20 -44 OR eGFR ≥ 45 and uACR ≥ 22.6 mg/mmol: Dapagliflozin OR Empagliflozin
texts.text: ·CKD if eGFR ≥ 20 -44 OR eGFR ≥ 45 and uACR ≥ 22.6 mg/mmol: Dapagliflozin OR Empagliflozin

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · BP control: if urine ACR <70 aim for clinic BP <140/90, if urine ACR ≥70 aim for clinic BP <130/80
texts.text: BP control: if urine ACR <70 aim for clinic BP <140/90, if urine ACR ≥70 aim for clinic BP <130/80

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ·CKD due to diabetic nephropathy ( T2DM only ) and eGFR ≥ 25 and ACR ≥ 3
texts.text: ·CKD due to diabetic nephropathy ( T2DM only ) and eGFR ≥ 25 and ACR ≥ 3

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: •Start at 10mg OD and titrate to 20mg OD where possible
texts.text: •Start at 10mg OD and titrate to 20mg OD where possible

texts.text: Step 3: Timely referral  to secondary care of  at-risk groups

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: Higher levels of proteinuria (uACR ≥ 70mg/mmol, uPCR ≥ 100mg/mmol) unless known to be due to diabetes and already appropriately treated

texts.text: Offer blood and urine testing for adults with high risk for CKD: diabetes, hypertension, previous AKI, CVD, urinary tract disease, incidental detection of haematuria or proteinuria, family history of renal disease, on nephrotoxic agents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Code CKD diagnosis on electronic patients' record based on eGFR (G1-5) and urine ACR (A1-3)
texts.text: Code CKD diagnosis on electronic patients' record based on eGFR (G1-5) and urine ACR (A1-3)

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: If ACR < 70 mg/mmol: aim for clinic BP < 140/90 mmHg

texts.text: If ACR ≥ 70 mg/mmol: aim for clinic BP < 130/80 mmHg

texts.text: Offer an ARB or ACE inhibitor (titrated to highest licensed dose tolerated)

texts.orig: Calculate 5-year risk of needing replacement therapy using 4-variable Kidney Failure Risk Equation - www.kfre.co.uk (if eGFR ≤60 ml/min/1.73m 2 )
texts.text: Calculate 5-year risk of needing replacement therapy using 4-variable Kidney Failure Risk Equation - www.kfre.co.uk (if eGFR ≤60 ml/min/1.73m 2 )

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · uACR ≥ 70 mg/mmol, unless known to be caused by diabetes and already appropriately treated
texts.text: uACR ≥ 70 mg/mmol, unless known to be caused by diabetes and already appropriately treated

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A sustained decrease in eGFR of 25% or more and a change in eGFR category within 12 months
texts.text: A sustained decrease in eGFR of 25% or more and a change in eGFR category within 12 months

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A sustained decrease in eGFR of 15 m/min/1.73m 2  or more per year
texts.text: A sustained decrease in eGFR of 15 m/min/1.73m 2  or more per year

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Hypertension that remains poorly controlled despite the use of at least 4 antihypertensive medicines
texts.text: Hypertension that remains poorly controlled despite the use of at least 4 antihypertensive medicines

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Known or suspected rare or genetic cause of CKD
texts.text: Known or suspected rare or genetic cause of CKD

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CKD stage 3 or 4 (with albuminuria) associated with type 2 diabetes
texts.text: CKD stage 3 or 4 (with albuminuria) associated with type 2 diabetes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Offer Finerenone* as an add-on if o n maximally tolerated RASi and SGLT2 inhibitor, unless unsuitable or contraindicated
texts.text: Offer Finerenone* as an add-on if o n maximally tolerated RASi and SGLT2 inhibitor, unless unsuitable or contraindicated

texts.label: text
texts.orig: NB: * please refer to local  NAPC Finerenone  prescribing guidelines
texts.text: NB: * please refer to local  NAPC Finerenone  prescribing guidelines

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Increase the dose if do not achieve > 40% reduction in nonHDL cholesterol and eGFR > 30 ml/min
texts.text: Increase the dose if do not achieve > 40% reduction in nonHDL cholesterol and eGFR > 30 ml/min

table_cells.text: What is CKD? CKD is the presence of one of the following for>3 months.
Who to Test Offer Screening for CKD using eGFR, serum creatinine and Urine Albumin: Creatinine

Ratio (UACR) to people with any of the following risk factors: • All people living with diabetes at least annually. • For those with an eGFR<60ml/min/1.73m2 a UACR • Hypertension-annually as part of hypertensions reviews https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/hypertension/diagnosis/investigations/ • Cardiovascular disease (ischaemic heart disease, chronic arterial disease or cerebral vascular disease) annually • History of acute kidney injury (monitor yearly for 3 years baseline) • Structural renal tract disease, recurrent renal calculi • Multi-system disease e.g., Systemic lupus erythematosus, • Family history of end-stage kidney disease (GFR category disease • Gout • Haematuria /Proteinuria (opportunistic detection) • Treated • Albuminuria (UACR ≥3 mg/mmol) confirmed on an early morning urine sample if UACR <70mg/mmol. • Urine sediment abnormalities e.g., presence of red (could indicate glomerular disease) or white blood cells (could indicate interstitial nephritis or infection e.g. pyelonephritis) , tubular epithelial cells (could indicate parenchymal disease) • Electrolyte and other abnormalities due to tubular disorders • Abnormalities detected by histology. • Structural abnormalities detected by imaging. • History of kidney transplantation eGFR of <60 ml/min/1.73 m2 (eGFR categories G3a-G5)

should be requested heart failure, peripheral as part of routine reviews even if function back to or prostatic hypertrophy vasculitis, myeloma G5) or hereditary kidney (NSAIDs, Lithium, Calcineurin inhibitors, KFRE (Kidney Failure Risk Equation) The Kidney Failure Risk Equation Healthcare professionals can use the Kidney failure risk equation to determine 2 and 5 year risk of treated kidney failure (dialysis and transplantation) for a patient with CKD stage 3a-5 There are also videos available on this website to explain risk to people living with CKD www.kidneyfailurerisk.co.uk NB: KFRE must be calculated using eGFR

Aminosalicylates etc) Urine Albumin: Creatinine Ratio (UACR) and CKD Diagnosis UACR is a useful marker of renal damage and complication risk. It is the usual method of assessing proteinuria. A confirmed (repeated) UACR>3mg/mmol represents proteinuria which is clinically significant. Measure UACR in all patients of CKD regardless of urine dipstick.
Markers of Kidney Damage (one or more) Decrease eGFR

Every patient at the time of a clinician diagnosing CKD should have a urine dipstick because haematuria raises possibility of systemic renal disease or structural renal abnormalities which needs further assessment. Haematuria 1. Use dipstick reagent strips rather than urine microscopy. 2. Evaluate further if a result of 1+ or more (initially repeat dipstick in 2 weeks) 3. Result is not useful if the person is menstruating if someone has a catheter or has a

UACR<3mg/mmol UACR 3-70mg/mmol (Confirm with subsequent early morning sample) UACR>70mg/mmol (No need to repeat the sample) eGFR>60ml/min/1.73m 2 Continue to screen as recommended by co- morbidities. eGFR<60ml/min/1.73m 2 or <90 ml/min/1.73m 2 and other markers of kidney damage CKD diagnosis - inform patient, signpost to patient resources, check eGFR if not already done and add coding for CKD (detailed G#A#). Manage CKD as per guideline and make referrals as needed. Check eGFR

table_cells.text: blockade and Optimise Lipids Start **ACE-inhibitor or ***ARB in the following populations: 1. Adults with hypertension and an ACR>30mg/mmol (category A3 or above) 2. Adults with diabetes and an ACR>3mg/mmol (category A2) 3. Adults without diabetes and ACR>70mg/mmol (also refer to nephrology) Titrate to maximum tolerated licensed dose ( NICE, NG203 ) Ideally do this within one month (see rapid titration protocol for RAAS blockade below) Atorvastatin 20mg once daily should be offered as initial therapy for primary and secondary prevention and national guidelines followed for review and titration. Optimise lipid lowering therapies according to national lipid lowering guidance NHS Accelerated Access Collaborative » Summary of
****Start SGLT-2 inhibitors SGLT-2 inhibitors in CKD and T2DM ' guidance)

( Refer to ' Clinical Pathway for the use of Person with Type 2 Diabetes Start Canagliflozin 100mg once daily ensuring the person has eGFR 30- 90ml/min/1.73m 2 OR Start Dapagliflozin OR Empagliflozin 10mg once daily ensuring the person has an eGFR 20-90ml/min/1.73m2 Note that glycaemic benefits will be limited at an eGFR <45ml/min/1.73m 2
Optimise Blood Pressure and Other Cardiovascular Risk Factors

national guidance for lipid management (england.nhs.uk) If the patient is young (below 40 years) and has CKD -Use QRISK ® 3-lifetime cardiovascular risk calculator: QRISK3-lifetime Stop nephrotoxic medications : Advise against use of NSAID's and discuss alternatives.

Person without Type 2 Diabetes Start Dapagliflozin OR Empagliflozin 10mg once daily ensuring the person has either: 1. An eGFR 20 to <45ml/min/1.73m 2 OR 2. An eGFR 45ml -90ml/min/1.73m and UACR ≥ 22.6mg/mmol

Initiate further blood pressure agents to treat to target • No diabetes or proteinuria- Target <140/90mmHg • UACR < 70mg/mmol: <130/80mmHg • UACR>70mg/mmol: Ideally <130-120/80mmHg taking into consideration frailty and co-morbidities. Caution in the elderly/frail - consider reviewing the targets Encourage home monitoring of Blood Pressure (NB targets are 5mmHg lower for HBPM) In those who have had a cardiovascular event, ensure offered aspirin with appropriate gastric protection (in some cases a H2 receptor antagonist may be preferred e.g., if having electrolyte abnormalities or in the instance of acute interstitial nephritis (ANI). Famotidine is the H2 receptor antagonist of choice in this situation). Aspirin may be considered for primary prevention in those at high cardiovascular risk. Initiation should be balanced with consideration of the increased bleeding risk, including thrombocytopathy with low eGFR. In those with established CAD or PAD at high risk of ischaemic events (see NICE) consider 2.5mg bd rivaroxaban alongside aspirin. Only if eGFR>15ml/min.

Follow the guidance in the document 'Clinical Pathway for the use of SGLT-2 inhibitors in Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) and Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) ' We would not advocate switching SGLT2i's so in those already established (including those on Canagliflozin) we would advise they continue and those already established on empagliflozin 25mg once daily should continue unless indicated to drop dose. Specialist initiation only if history of: transplantation; on immunological therapy; polycystic kidney disease; haemodialysis. ****The least expensive option of the available treatments should be used. Generic dapagliflozin is the most cost effective and the preferred SGLT2 inhibitor locally

grid.text: ****Start SGLT-2 inhibitors SGLT-2 inhibitors in CKD and T2DM ' guidance)
****Start SGLT-2 inhibitors SGLT-2 inhibitors in CKD and T2DM ' guidance)
Optimise Blood Pressure and Other Cardiovascular Risk Factors
